content:
Ensemble ideas trace to **Schapire** (1990) and **Freund & Schapire's AdaBoost** (1996), which reweighted misclassified examples so later weak classifiers focused on hard cases.

**Jerome Friedman** unified boosting with gradient descent in the loss space: **Gradient Boosting Machines** (1999 technical report; expanded 2001) showed that fitting trees to **pseudo-residuals**—negative loss gradients—generalizes AdaBoost's reweighting to arbitrary differentiable losses.

**Friedman** also introduced **shrinkage** (learning rate) as a regularizer for GBM. The 2000s brought practical software: **R's gbm** package and commercial tools spread GBM for actuarial and credit modeling.

The 2010s saw industrial-scale implementations—**XGBoost** (Chen & Guestrin, 2016) and **LightGBM** (Ke et al., 2017)—that kept the sequential residual-fitting core while adding histogram splits, sparsity-aware optimization, and regularization tuned for massive tabular datasets and Kaggle competitions.

timeline.label: AdaBoost (1996)
timeline.description: Sequential reweighting of misclassified points; foundational boosting intuition before the gradient formulation.

timeline.label: Gradient Boosting Machine (1999–2001)
timeline.description: Friedman frames boosting as gradient descent: each tree fits pseudo-residuals from the loss gradient.

timeline.label: Shrinkage & stochastic variants
timeline.description: Learning-rate shrinkage and subsampling stabilize GBM and reduce overfitting on noisy tabular data.

timeline.label: XGBoost & LightGBM era
timeline.description: Optimized libraries make gradient boosting the default strong baseline for structured ML at scale.
